segments.s775-g33: چو introduces a time clause meaning 'when'. او is the third-person pronoun 'he'. کشته شد is a light passive construction: کشته (past participle 'killed') + شد (auxiliary 'became/was') → 'was killed'. پادشاهی گرفت is a simple past clause where گرفت (past of گرفتن 'to take/seize') means 'he took/assumed kingship' (پادشاهی = 'kingship'). Word order is typical Persian: temporal clause (چو ... شد) followed by the main action (پادشاهی گرفت).
segments.s775-g34: بدین گونه is an adverbial phrase 'in this manner' (بدین = به + این). ناپارسایی 'impiety / moral unworthiness' is the object/thing taken on. گرفت (past of گرفتن) again means 'he took on' or 'assumed' — here figuratively 'assumed impiety'. The clause states a consecutive result: 'thus he assumed impiety.'
segments.s775-g35: طلایه 'vanguard' is the subject. همی‌گوید is a present/progressive habitual verb form 'keeps saying / reports' (همی‑ prefix marks continuous or repeated action in classical Persian). آمد is a past form 'has come' or 'came', and سپاه 'army' is the noun being reported; the phrase literally reads 'the vanguard keeps saying,
segments.s775-g36: نباید here is impersonal 'it must not / ought not' and introduces a prohibition clause with the subordinator که ('that'). بر ما بگیرند راه is a subordinate clause in which بگیرند (subjunctive/present plural of گرفتن) means 'they take/seize' and بر...راه means 'take the road against us' or 'seize the way toward us' (بر = 'against / upon' + ما 'us', راه = 'road'). The structure is: 'it ought not be that they seize the road against us.'
segments.s775-g37: چو 'when' introduces the temporal clause. بدخواه جنگی combines بدخواه 'ill-wisher / hostile one' with جنگی 'warlike / fighter' (an attributive compound meaning 'a hostile warrior'). به بالین رسید is a locative phrase: به 'to/toward' + بالین 'bedside/pillow' and رسید 'arrived/reached' — 'reached the bedside' (i.e., came to the place of the sleeping/lying person).
segments.s775-g38: نباید introduces prohibition 'you must not / should not.' تو را uses the 2nd-person pronoun with the object marker را for emphasis/definiteness. با سپاه 'with the army' is an instrumental/associative phrase (با = 'with'). آرمید is past of آرمیدن 'to rest/sleep/lie at rest'; in context it means 'you should not lie down/rest with the army.' The negative obligation is expressed impersonally with نباید plus the object clause.
segments.s775-g39: چنین گل 'such a flower' is a nominative phrase (چنین = 'such/so', گل = 'flower' often figurative). به پالیز شاهان 'to/for the kings' orchard' locates the flower (به = 'to/for', پالیز = 'orchard/field', شاهان = 'kings'). مباد is an optative negative 'may it not be' (poetic wish/benediction). Together: 'May such a flower not be in the kings' orchard.'
segments.s775-g40: چو باشد is a conditional/temporal 'when/if it is' (چو = 'when/if', باشد = present/subjunctive 'be / is'). نیاید 'does not come / will not appear' is a negative present/future form. ز پالیز یاد 'remembrance from the orchard' (ز = 'from', پالیز = 'orchard', یاد = 'memory/remembrance'). The clause means 'when that happens, no remembrance will come from the orchard,' i.e., the place will leave no memory or renown.
segments.s775-g73: غمی شد is a compact clause meaning “sorrow arose/there was sorrow,” with شد (past of شدن) functioning as the intransitive verb “became/occurred.” برابر here is an adverb/prepositional element meaning “opposite/against,” introducing the following nominal phrase; صفی in this poetic context stands for a formed “rank/line” (an instance of the noun صف with the -ی ending) and the subject is left implicit. برکشید is the simple past (3rd person singular) of برکشیدن and means “lifted/drew up” (or, in a martial context, “drew [a weapon]”); the clause order and omitted pronoun are typical of Ferdowsi’s compressed, poetic syntax, so the two clauses are best read as consecutive events: sorrow arose, then a line (or someone) drew up opposite.
segments.s775-g74: هوا نیلگون شد is a standard predicative structure: noun + adjective + شد, i.e. “the air/sky became blue/indigo.” زمین ناپدید omits an explicit copula (also common in poetry) and uses ناپدید as a predicative adjective/participle meaning “vanished” — read here as “the earth (was) vanished / the earth disappeared.” The sequence links as two brief proclamations of cosmic change.
segments.s775-g17: به مرو اندرون is a prepositional phrase meaning “into/within Merv” (به + place + اندرون). رزم کردم is a periphrastic verbal expression where the noun رزم (‘battle’) combines with the past tense of کردن (کردم) to mean “I fought.” سه روز is a duration phrase (‘three days’) placed after the verb for emphasis (poetic word order).
segments.s775-g18: چهارم here means “on the fourth (day).” چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” بفروخت is a 3rd‑person past form (classical/poetic shape with the b‑ prefix) whose literal sense is “parted with / sold” and is used here with گیتی‌فروز (an epithet) as its subject; the exact referent and nuance are context‑dependent, so the clause reads “when the world‑illuminator parted with it.”
segments.s775-g19: شدم تنگدل uses شد (past of شدن) + adjective تنگدل to mean “I became sorrowful/heavy‑hearted.” رزم کردم repeats the periphrastic construction “I fought.” درشت follows the verb and modifies the manner of fighting (“fiercely / roughly”); here درشت functions adverbially.
segments.s775-g20: جفاپیشه ماهوی is a nominal phrase: جفاپیشه (cruel/oppressor) qualifies ماهوی (a personal epithet/name or adjective ‘mahwī’). بنمود is the simple past “showed/turned,” and پشت is the direct noun “back”; together بنمود پشت = “turned/showed his back.” Word order is adjective + (epithet) + verb + object.
segments.s775-g21: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” ماهوی here is the subject (same epithet as before). گنج خداوند خویش is a possessive noun phrase: گنج ‘treasure’ modified by خداوند خویش ‘his own lord’ (خویش is the reflexive/possessive element). In the manuscript the possessive ش is separated, but syntactically it attaches to خوی (classical poetic spacing).
segments.s775-g22: بیاورد is a 3rd‑person past tense “he brought.” بی‌رنج is an adverbial phrase “without toil/effort.” و links the coordinated verbs; بنهاد is another past verb “he placed/put down.” پیش functions as a locative/adverb “before/in front (of us).” The clause describes two consecutive actions (brought and placed) done without trouble.
segments.s775-g23: چو introduces the temporal clause “when.” آگنده شد is a compound past (آگنده + شد) meaning “became filled.” مرد بی‌تن is the subject phrase “the man without a body” (بی‌تن functioning as a descriptive adjective). به چیز marks the thing with which he was filled — here literally “with a thing/matter.”
segments.s775-g24: مرا is the first‑person object (“to me” / “me”). خود تو is emphatic subject order: تو with خود gives “you yourself.” گفتی is the main past verb “you said/told.” ندیدست is reported perfect/past nominal meaning “has not been seen / had not seen” and here functions as the content of what was told; نیز adds “also/too.” The clause therefore reports: “you yourself told me that he had not seen it either.”
segments.s775-g65: سپاهی بکشتی برآمد ز آب is an SOV-style clause: the subject سپاهی (an army/warriors) is followed by the adverbial بکشتی (“by ship/in ships”), then the past verb برآمد (“emerged/rose up”) and the source phrase ز آب (“from the water”). بکشتی here is an adverbial compound (ب- + کشتی) meaning ‘by ship’.
segments.s775-g66: که introduces a result/cause clause (‘because/so that’). از گرد پیدا نبود آفتاب is literal ‘from (of) dust the sun was not visible’: از + گرد = ‘from/out of the dust’; پیدا نبود is the past negative of بودن (‘was not visible’). The whole clause explains the effect (dust so thick that the sun could not be seen).
segments.s775-g67: سپهدار بیژن به پیش سپاه is a noun phrase with title + name (سپهدار بیژن = ‘the commander Bijan’). به پیش سپاه is a locative/ directional phrase ‘to/in front of the army’ (به = to/toward; پیش = before/in front of; سپاه = the army).
segments.s775-g68: بیامد is the past simple ‘came’. که سازد همی رزمگاه is a purpose/result clause: که + present/subjunctive سازد = ‘so that he may prepare/make’, while همی is an archaic emphatic/continuous particle (here strengthening the verb) and رزمگاه is the object ‘battlefield’. Together: ‘he came to set up the battlefield.’
segments.s775-g69: چو is a temporal conjunction here (‘when’). ماهوی سوری functions as a descriptive epithet before the subject: a compound epithet (moon-like / red/ Syrian — poetic epithet) and سپه را is the definite object with the object marker را. بدید is the past tense ‘saw’. The clause thus reads ‘When the [epithet] saw the army…’.
segments.s775-g70: تو گفتی literal ‘you said’ (تو = you). که introduces reported content (‘that’), followed by جانش ز تن برپرید: جانش = ‘his life/soul’ (with enclitic possessive), ز تن = ‘from the body’, and برپرید is a past verb meaning ‘leapt up/ flew away’ (a poetic way to say the soul left the body).
segments.s775-g71: ز بس ... lists the cause/quantity: ز بس = ‘from/for so many’ (i.e. ‘because of so many’). جوشن و خود و زرین سپر is a coordinated list of items (‘cuirasses and khud and golden shields’). The conjunction و links the items; زرین is an adjective ‘golden’ modifying سپر. The phrase is a verbless noun-list clause functioning adverbially.
segments.s775-g72: This is a parallel continuation introduced again by ز بس (‘by reason of so many’), followed by a coordinated list نیزه و گرز و چاچی‌تبر (‘spears and maces and broad-axes’). The repetition of ز بس is a poetic device to emphasize the abundance of weaponry.
segments.s775-g9: بدو here is a prepositional/dative clitic meaning 'to him' placed before the verb; گفت is the past verb 'said' (listed senses did not include 'said', so treated as the speech verb); برسام is the addressee's name; کای شهریار is a vocative phrase 'O king' (کای = vocative 'O', شهریار = 'king'). The order is: indirect object + verb + name + vocative.
segments.s775-g10: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'; من is the subject 'I'; بردم is the 1st‑person singular past 'I took/brought'; از چاچ marks origin 'from Chach'; چندان سوار is a quantified noun phrase 'so many horsemen' (چندان modifying سوار). Word order follows the older Persian pattern with the temporal marker and subject before the verb and origin phrase after the verb.
segments.s775-g11: بیاوردم is the 1st‑person past 'I brought'; از مرو gives the source 'from Merv'; چندان qualifies the following noun as 'so many'; بنه is an archaic/uncertain noun here (likely 'bands' or 'troops'), so its precise sense is unclear. The clause states the quantity brought from Merv.
segments.s775-g12: بشد is an older/periphrastic past meaning 'it happened/then' or 'thereupon (he) became/appeared' (here read as 'then appeared'); یزدگرد is the proper name subject; از میان is the prepositional phrase 'from among'; یک‌تنه is an adverbial 'single‑handed/alone'. Together the clause describes Yazdegerd appearing alone from among them as the result.
segments.s775-g13: تو را is the dative/indirect object 'to you' (تو marked by را for emphasis/accusative/dative); گفته functions as the reportive 'it is said/has been said' (listed senses lacked 'said', so marked unknown); the following nominal phrase تخت زرین اوی gives the reported content 'his golden throne' (اوی = 'his'). The particle بد before تخت is archaic or uncertain here and likely intensifies or introduces the reported noun; its exact meaning is ambiguous.
segments.s775-g14: همان is the demonstrative 'that very' or 'the same'; یاره is a noun 'companion/beloved' (here appositive to the throne clause); گوهرآگین is a compound adjective 'gem‑adorned' modifying یاره; اوی is the possessive 'his/her' attached after the noun. The phrase is an appositive repeating/clarifying what is said to you.
segments.s775-g15: همان gنج و تاجش is a coordinated noun phrase 'that same treasure and his crown' (تاجش = تاج + possessive ش 'his'); فرستم is the 1st‑person present/future 'I will send' or 'I send' (expressing intention); به چاج marks the destination/recipient 'to Chaj' (چاج here treated as a proper name). The object(s) precede the verb due to topical order.
segments.s775-g16: تو را باید is an impersonal/modal construction meaning 'you ought/should' with تو را marked by را (emphatic/dative); اندر جهان is the locative 'in the world'; تخت عاج is the noun phrase 'ivory throne' (تخت = throne, عاج = ivory). The clause expresses that an ivory throne in the world is fitting/appropriate for you.
segments.s775-g57: The phrase begins with the prepositional pronoun 'بدو' (to him/unto him) followed by 'گفت' (said); the speaker 'برسام' comes after the verb, so the literal order is 'to him said Bersam'. 'کای شهریار' is a vocative: 'کای' = 'O' addressing 'شهریار' = 'sovereign/king'.
segments.s775-g58: 'سرآمد' is a predicate/adjective meaning 'the foremost' or 'preeminent'. 'برین' here is the fused demonstrative (بر + این) meaning 'this' or 'of this'; 'تخمه' means 'seed' or 'offspring' (i.e. lineage). 'بر روزگار' is a prepositional phrase 'in/on the times/world' (بر = on/upon, روزگار = the age/times), completing the sense 'foremost of this lineage in the age.'
segments.s775-g59: 'بران' (on/over those) points back to previously mentioned towns; it governs 'شهرها' (the cities). 'تازیان' is the plural noun 'the Arabs', and 'راست‌دست' qualifies them — here describing their position or disposition as 'on the right hand / right‑handed (right flank)'. Word order: locative phrase + subject + modifier.
segments.s775-g60: The particle 'که' introduces the result/explanatory clause. The clause uses the parallel negation 'نه ... نه ...' (neither ... nor ...): 'نه شاه ماند نه یزدان‌پرست' = 'neither a king remained nor a God‑worshipper remained.' 'ماند' is the past of ماندن ('remained / was left'). The verb follows the first nominal in the typical Persian order.
segments.s775-g61: 'چو' is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. 'بشنید' is the past tense 'he heard'. The subject 'بیژن' follows and is followed by the object 'سپه' (army) and the verb 'برگرفت' (took up / mustered). So the clause reads: 'When (he) heard, Bijan took up the army.' Note the classical word order inversion and the compound verb برگرفت (بر + گرفت) = 'took up/raised'.
segments.s775-g62: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from / because of' governing 'کار جهان' = 'the affairs/work of the world' (literally 'worldly affairs'). The phrase 'دست بر سر گرفت' is idiomatic: 'he put his hand on his head' (expressing despair, grief, or agitation). Word order places 'دست' (hand) before the prepositional phrase 'بر سر' and finishes with the past verb 'گرفت' (took).
segments.s775-g63: 'طلایه بیامد' = 'the vanguard came' (طلایه = vanguard; بیامد = came). The particle 'که' then links to a second clause 'آمد سپاه' where the verb 'آمد' precedes the subject 'سپاه' (the army) — a stylistic repetition: 'the vanguard came, that (indeed) the army came.'
segments.s775-g64: This clause describes the preparations: 'به پیکند سازد همی رزمگاه.' 'به پیکند' (by/with messengers / he sent messengers) uses 'پیکند' as 'send a messenger / bring news'. 'سازد' is the classical third‑person present/past-stem meaning 'he makes/arranges' and together with 'رزمگاه' (battlefield) yields 'he prepares the battlefield.' 'همی' is an emphatic/continuous particle (classical Persian) marking the action.
segments.s775-g49: به پیکار ما is a prepositional phrase meaning “to/against our fighting” (به + noun + possessor ما = “against our battle/us in battle”). پیش آرد سپاه places the verb آرد (“brings/advances”) before its subject سپاه (“the army/host”), a common poetic word order (verb before subject); here آرد is a narrative/present-tense form meaning ‘advances/puts forward’. The whole segment therefore means “brings/advances its army against our fight.”
segments.s775-g50: مگر here is a rhetorical interrogative (“is it not that / shall we not”) introducing the question. باز خواهیم is a first‑person plural verbal phrase (خواهیم = “we will/would/shall” + باز “back/again/repay”) and زو is the contracted poetic form of از او (“from him”); کین شاه is the object “the king’s vengeance/hatred.” Together the clause asks, in effect, “shall we not repay/requite the king’s vengeance upon him?”
segments.s775-g51: ازان پس is a temporal phrase “after that” (ازان = “from/thereupon” + پس = “after/then”). بپرسید is an instruction/optative form here (classically used as an imperative/2pl polite: “ask”) and کز introduces a relative clause “which/of whom.” The segment therefore means “after that, ask which (of the) renowned (men).”
segments.s775-g52: که introduces the subordinate clause (“who/that”); ماند is the verb “remains/is left.” ایچ is a negating/emphatic word meaning “nothing/not at all,” modifying فرزند (“descendant/son”), so ماند ایچ فرزند = “no son remains.” کاید به کار is a verbless/elliptical phrase meaning roughly “to take up the task / to be fit for the work”; the precise lexical sense of کاید is archaic here. Overall: “who remains, since no son remains to do the work.”
segments.s775-g53: جهاندارشه (“the world‑ruler/sovereign”) is the topic marked by را (object/topic marker). برادر is the noun “brother” and بدست here means “into the hand(s) / entrusted (to).” The short phrase is elliptical—“the sovereign’s brother (is to be) put into [someone’s] hands”—and depends on the following conditional context to complete the sense.
segments.s775-g54: پسر‌گر is a conditional construction (پسر + گر ‘if there is a son’); نبود is the negative past/optative of بودن (“is not/there is not” here functioning as ‘if there is not’), and ایچ intensifies the negation (“at all/nothing”). دختر بدست = “the daughter (is) in hand/entrusted.” Together the clause reads: “if there is no son at all, (then) the daughter is entrusted.”
segments.s775-g55: که here introduces a purpose/result clause (“so that/that”). او را is the direct object “him” (او + object marker را). بیاریم is a first‑person plural subjunctive/optative form meaning “let us bring” (the given lemma/senses are mismatched lexically, but grammatically this is the jussive/subjunctive 'we bring/let us bring’), و یاری دهیم = “and let us give aid/help” (یاری = help, دهیم = ‘we give’).
segments.s775-g56: به ماهوی is a prepositional phrase; به = “to/for,” ماهوی is an abstract/qualifying term here (sense labeled “essential/ontological” in the lexical list) modifying the following phrase. بر کامگاری دهیم literally “we give for/unto fulfilment/success” (بر = on/toward, کامگاری = fulfilment/success, دهیم = ‘we give’). The whole segment expresses purpose/result: “to grant him, in essence, fulfilment/success.”
segments.s775-g25: 'به مرو اندرون بود لشکر دو ماه' has an archaic word order: the locative phrase 'به مرو اندرون' = 'into/inside Merv' (preposition + place + 'اندرون' = inside) comes before the verb 'بود' (past 'was/there was'). 'لشکر' is the subject 'an army' and 'دو ماه' is a duration phrase 'for two months' placed after the noun. Overall: 'In Merv inside was an army for two months.'
segments.s775-g26: 'به خوبی' is an adverbial phrase 'kindly/well' formed with 'به' + noun; 'نکرد' is the past negative 'did not do' (here negating the verb). The enclitic/emphatic negator 'ایچ' = 'not at all' intensifies the negation. 'بر ما نگاه' is a prepositional/oblique phrase 'toward us, a glance' functioning as the thing not given — 'did not cast any kind glance upon us.'
segments.s775-g27: Here the verb 'بکشت' (archaic past) precedes the pronoun 'او' — an older word order where verb + subject appear together. 'خداوند را' is the definite object with the object marker 'را'. The locative/adverbial 'در نهان' = 'in secret/secretly' follows to indicate manner.
segments.s775-g28: 'چنان' is a modifier meaning 'so/such' and directly modifies the noun phrase 'پادشاهی بزرگ جهان' = 'a great kingship/kingdom of the world'. Adjective 'بزرگ' follows the head noun 'پادشاهی' in this poetic order, and 'جهان' is the genitive/qualifying word 'of the world.'
segments.s775-g29: 'سواری که گفتی میان سپاه' is a relative clause: 'سواری' (the rider/horseman) + 'که' (who/whom) introduces the clause. 'گفتی' is the verb 'you said' (second person past) and 'میان سپاه' is a locative phrase 'in the middle of the army' describing where he was.
segments.s775-g30: 'همی‌برگذارد' contains the old habitual/continuous prefix 'همی' + verb 'برگذارد' giving a habitual or iterative sense ('would/kept [doing]'). The verb governs the following noun 'سر' (head) as its object; 'از' marks origin/source and 'چرخ ماه' is a poetic image 'the wheel/disk of the moon' — together: 'he would repeatedly lift his head from/above the moon's disk.'
segments.s775-g31: The preposition 'ز' (poetic for 'from/among') + 'ترکان' marks the group 'from the Turks.' 'کسی' with the negative verb 'نرفت' yields 'no one went/stepped forward.' 'پیش' = 'before/in front of' and 'گرزش' is a noun meaning his blow/club-strike; the verb 'نرفت' (past negative of 'رفت') states that nobody advanced before his blow.
segments.s775-g32: This clause is compact/poetic. 'همی' gives an ongoing/iterative or emphatic tone ('thus/so' or 'indeed'); 'ز' + 'و' here function together as the fused preposition+pronoun 'from him' (classical 'زو' = 'از او'). 'دل نامداران' = 'the hearts of the renowned/illustrious ones' (دل = hearts, نامداران = famous men). The final verb 'بکفت' is archaic/poetic and here indicates a state/result concerning those hearts (rendered in the translation as 'were taken/overthrown' — the exact nuance is archaic and not fully covered by the provided sense list).
segments.s775-g1: This clause means 'thus, when the news reached Bijan.' چنين is an adverb 'thus/so' that opens the clause; تا + رسید here functions temporally — 'when/until ... reached' (an older/poetic use of تا meaning 'when'). به indicates direction or recipient ('to/for'), followed by the proper name بیژن (Bijan). رسید is a past/perfective verb 'reached/arrived' and آگهی is the noun 'news/information' that is the thing which 'reached' him.
segments.s775-g2: که introduces a subordinate content clause 'that ...'. ماهوی here functions as a proper name/title (not the abstract 'essential' sense), so its lexical meaning is not covered in the provided sense list. بگرفت is an epic/past form of گرفتن ('seized/took') with the verbal prefix بـ; تخت مهی is the object phrase 'the splendid/beautiful throne' where مهی is a postpositive adjective qualifying تخت.
segments.s775-g3: بهر سو is a fixed directional phrase 'toward that side' (بهر = toward/for, سو = side/direction). فرستاد is the past tense 'sent/dispatched.' مهر و نگین are the items sent: مهر here is a physical seal/signet (not 'love'), and نگین is 'gem' or signet-stone.
segments.s775-g4: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle (here functioning like 'so/that' or 'thereby'); رام گردد is a present-subjunctive/optative-like periphrasis 'may become/be made tame' (گردد = 'become'). برو is an adverb/particle here implying motion toward/coming (not the simple imperative 'go'), and بر زمین literally 'onto the ground' (بر = on/upon, زمین = ground/earth). Together the clause expresses purpose/result: '(so) that he might be tamed and come down to the ground.'
segments.s775-g5: کنون = 'now.' سوی جیحون is 'toward the Jeyhun' (سوی = toward, جیحون = the Amu Darya river). نهادست is an archaic/perfect form of نهادن ('has placed' / 'set') and combines here with روی ('face') in the idiom روی نهادن — 'to set one's face toward' i.e. 'to turn/face toward.' Grammatically this is a compound perfective sense 'has set his face toward.'
segments.s775-g6: به پرخاش is a prepositional phrase meaning 'for/with the intent of attack' (پرخاش here = aggression/assault rather than mere reproach). با introduces accompaniment 'with,' لشکری is 'an army/host' (troops), and جنگجوی describes the army as 'warlike/warrior' — together: 'with a warlike army for assault.'
segments.s775-g7: بپرسید is a past tense verb 'asked.' Here the subject is بیژن (Bijan): 'Bijan asked ...' The first که introduces an indirect question/relative 'who/which' (که = 'who'), تاجش is 'his crown' (تاج + possessive suffix), and the second که + داد is the verb phrase 'who gave.' So the whole clause: 'Bijan asked who had given his crown.'
segments.s775-g8: برو کرد here is an archaic sequence meaning roughly 'thereupon/did' (برو functions as a discourse particle 'thereupon' rather than the literal 'go'); کرد is the past 'did/made.' گوینده is 'the speaker/narrator' (here 'narrator' fits best), and آن کار یاد is the object phrase: آن کار 'that affair/deed' and یاد used as a verbal noun meaning 'mention/recall' (یاد کردن = to recall/mention). Together: 'the narrator thereupon mentioned/recalled that affair.'
segments.s775-g41: چو is the temporal subordinating particle 'when'. بشنید is a 3rd-person past ('he heard'); the clause places the time first (چو + past) as is common in classical Persian. سپه گرد کرد is a light-verb construction: سپه (army) + گرد کردن ('to gather/assemble'); گرد here is part of the verbal compound meaning 'to muster/gather' and کرد is the past auxiliary 'did/made' completing the action.
segments.s775-g42: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' indicating origin or composition. ترکان سواران is a genitival grouping 'Turkish horsemen' (ترکان modifying سواران). روز نبرد is a temporal/epithet phrase 'the day of battle' qualifying the horsemen as battle-ready or destined for the battle-day.
segments.s775-g43: ز is the preposition 'from'. قجقار is an onomatopoeic noun (a crackling/clattering sound); here it names the sound source. باشی is an archaic/uncertain item in this line (it can be read as part of a compound title or an old form) and is therefore left uncertain; بیامد is the past 'came', and دمان ('hem/skirt') is the noun that follows, so syntactically this phrase says 'from the clatter came the hem/ruffle' (a poetic way to report movement/noise).
segments.s775-g44: نجست here is the past-of-root 'جست' with the negative prefix ن- (so 'did not leap/ did not spring'). ایچ‌گونه is a deictic/adverbial intensifier 'in such a way / by no means' (used here to mean 'in no such manner'). بره is the noun 'lamb' used metaphorically, and بر زمان is locative/temporal 'upon/at the time' — together: 'no lamb leapt in that way at that moment.'
segments.s775-g45: چو is 'when'. نزدیک is an adverb/adjective 'near' (here an adverbial complement). شهر بخارا is the place-name 'the city of Bukhara' (شهر governed by نزدیک). رسید is the 3rd-person past 'arrived/reached' completing the temporal clause.
segments.s775-g46: همه modifies the following noun as 'the whole/entire'. دشت نخشب is the place-name 'the Nakhshab plain' (نخشب here is a toponym). سپه گسترید is a verbal phrase: سپه (army) is what is being spread/deployed and گسترید (past of گستراندن/گستردن) means 'spread out/deployed' — i.e. 'he spread his army across the plain.'
segments.s775-g47: به یاران is the indirect-object prepositional phrase 'to the companions'. چنین گفت که is the reporting formula 'he said thus/said that'. اکنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. شتاب is the noun 'haste' (here the content of the admonition: 'do not be in haste'). Grammatically this sets up a reported command following که.
segments.s775-g48: مدارید is a hortative/imperative sense 'refrain / be gentle / do not (hurry)' addressed to the companions (2nd-plural); تا introduces a purpose or limit clause 'so that / until'. او is 'he'. ب + دین together form بدین ('by this / in this way') — here the components are written separately in the segmentation but function as the demonstrative/adverbial 'by this/this way'. روی آب is 'to/on the water' or 'toward the water' — together: 'do not hurry now until he comes to the water by this route.'

sentences.s775-s17: When he was slain, he took the throne; in this way he assumed impiety.
sentences.s775-s18: The vanguard keeps reporting, 'An army has come'; they must not seize the road against us.
sentences.s775-s19: When a hostile fighter reached the bedside, you must not rest with the army.
sentences.s775-s20: May such a flower never be in the kings' garden; when that is so, no memory will come from the orchard.
sentences.s775-s37: Sorrow arose; a line drew up opposite; the sky turned indigo and the earth vanished.
sentences.s775-s9: I fought inside Merv for three days; on the fourth, when the world‑illuminator parted with it.
sentences.s775-s10: I became heavy‑hearted; I fought fiercely; the cruel Mahvī turned his back.
sentences.s775-s11: When Mahvī brought his lord’s treasure without toil and set it before (us).
sentences.s775-s12: When the disembodied man was filled with matter, you yourself told me that he had not seen it either.
sentences.s775-s33: An army in ships rose up from the water, and from the dust the sun could not be seen.
sentences.s775-s34: Bijan the commander came before the host, in order to set up the battlefield.
sentences.s775-s35: When that moonlike/beautiful (one) saw the host, you said his life leapt from his body.
sentences.s775-s36: By reason of so many cuirasses and khud and golden shields, by reason of so many spears and maces and broad-axes—
sentences.s775-s5: Barsam said to him, "O sovereign, when I took from Chach so many horsemen..."
sentences.s775-s6: I brought from Merv so many bands (troops); Yazdegerd then came forth from among them single‑handed.
sentences.s775-s7: You have been told that his golden throne — and that same jewel‑adorned beloved — are his.
sentences.s775-s8: I will send that same treasure and his crown to Chaj; you should have an ivory throne in the world.
sentences.s775-s29: Bersam said to him, "O sovereign, you are the foremost scion of this lineage in the world."
sentences.s775-s30: Upon those cities were the Arabs on the right flank, so that neither king nor God‑worshipper remained.
sentences.s775-s31: When Bijan heard, he mustered the army; abandoning worldly affairs, he put his hand to his head.
sentences.s775-s32: The vanguard came, and the army arrived; he sent messengers and prepared the battlefield.
sentences.s775-s25: If he brings his host forward to fight us, shall we not in turn avenge the king's wrath on him?
sentences.s775-s26: After that, ask which of the renowned remains, since no son is left to take up the work.
sentences.s775-s27: If the world‑ruler has a brother, (let him be) put into the son's hands; if there is no son at all, the daughter is in hand.
sentences.s775-s28: that we bring him and give him aid, and thereby grant him success (fulfilment of desire).
sentences.s775-s13: In Merv’s inner quarters an army remained for two months; it did not cast a single kind glance toward us.
sentences.s775-s14: He killed the lord in secret and thus obtained so great a kingship over the world.
sentences.s775-s15: The horseman whom you mentioned, in the midst of the army, would lift his head above the moon’s disk.
sentences.s775-s16: None of the Turks stepped forward to meet his blow; thus the hearts of the renowned were taken (captivated/overthrown) by him.
sentences.s775-s1: Thus, when the news reached Bijan, that Māhuy had seized the splendid throne.
sentences.s775-s2: He sent a seal and a gem toward that side, so that (he) might be made tame and come down to the ground.
sentences.s775-s3: Now he has set his face toward the Jeyhun, bent on assault with a warlike host.
sentences.s775-s4: Bijan asked who had given his crown; the narrator then recalled that affair.
sentences.s775-s21: When Bijan heard, he mustered the troops — the Turkish horsemen for the day of battle.
sentences.s775-s22: From the clatter came the rustle of hems; no lamb leapt in such a way at that moment.
sentences.s775-s23: When he came near the city of Bukhara, he deployed the whole army across the Nakhshab plain.
sentences.s775-s24: He said to his companions: 'Don't hurry now; wait until he comes to the water by this route.'
